title: Chapter 1: Advanced Node And Express - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of Passport.js authentication, sessions, serialization, hashing, and middleware

questions.question: What is Passport.js primarily used for in a Node.js application?

options.text: Database management and ORM
Authentication middleware for handling login strategies
Template rendering and view engine management
Rate limiting and API throttling

questions.explanation: Passport.js is authentication middleware for Node.js. It supports over 500 authentication strategies including local (username/password), OAuth (Google, GitHub, Facebook), JWT, and more. It integrates seamlessly with Express applications.

questions.question: What is the purpose of serializeUser() in Passport.js?

options.text: It encrypts the user's password before storing it
It determines which data from the user object should be stored in the session
It converts the user object to JSON for API responses
It validates the user's credentials against the database

questions.explanation: serializeUser() determines what user data gets stored in the session. Typically, only the user ID is serialized to keep the session small: passport.serializeUser((user, done) => done(null, user.id)). The complementary deserializeUser() retrieves the full user object from the stored ID.

questions.question: Which npm package is commonly used to hash passwords in Node.js applications?

options.text: crypto-hash
bcrypt
md5
password-encrypt

questions.explanation: bcrypt is the standard library for hashing passwords in Node.js. It automatically handles salting and uses a deliberately slow hashing algorithm to resist brute-force attacks. bcrypt.hash(password, saltRounds) creates a hash, and bcrypt.compare() verifies passwords against stored hashes.

questions.question: What does the express-session middleware do?

options.text: It creates WebSocket connections between client and server
It manages server-side session data and sends a session ID cookie to the client
It logs all user sessions to a file
It limits the duration of HTTP connections

questions.explanation: express-session creates a session middleware that stores session data on the server and sends a session ID to the client as a cookie. The session object (req.session) persists data across requests. Options include secret (for signing the cookie), resave, and saveUninitialized.

questions.question: How should sensitive configuration values like API keys and database URIs be stored in a Node.js application?

options.text: Hardcoded directly in the source code
In a JSON configuration file committed to version control
In environment variables, typically using a .env file with the dotenv package
In HTML meta tags on the frontend

questions.explanation: Sensitive values should be stored in environment variables. The dotenv package loads variables from a .env file into process.env. The .env file should be added to .gitignore to prevent secrets from being committed to version control.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'done' callback in Passport.js strategies?

options.text: It sends the HTTP response to the client
It signals to Passport that the authentication verification is complete, passing the result (error, user, or failure)
It closes the database connection
It redirects the user to the home page

questions.explanation: The done callback follows the pattern done(error, user, info). Calling done(null, user) indicates success, done(null, false, { message: '...' }) indicates failure (invalid credentials), and done(err) indicates a server error. Passport uses this to proceed with the auth flow.

questions.question: Which middleware function checks if a user is authenticated before allowing access to a route?

options.text: A custom middleware that checks req.isAuthenticated()
passport.authorize()
express.requireAuth()
session.verify()

questions.explanation: Passport adds the req.isAuthenticated() method to the request object. A common pattern is creating an ensureAuthenticated middleware: function(req, res, next) { if (req.isAuthenticated()) return next(); res.redirect('/login'); }. This middleware is then used to protect routes.

questions.question: In a Passport.js local strategy, what credentials does it verify by default?

options.text: API key and secret
Username and password from the request body
JWT token from the Authorization header
OAuth access token

questions.explanation: The passport-local strategy authenticates users with a username and password submitted via a form (in the request body). By default, it looks for fields named 'username' and 'password', but these can be customized via the usernameField and passwordField options.

questions.question: What is the purpose of passport.initialize() middleware?

options.text: It creates the login form HTML
It initializes Passport and sets up the basics for handling authentication on each request
It generates encryption keys for passwords
It connects to the OAuth provider

questions.explanation: passport.initialize() is required middleware that initializes Passport on every request. It sets up the req._passport property. It must be used before passport.session() in the middleware stack. Without it, Passport authentication will not function.

questions.question: When implementing social authentication (e.g., GitHub OAuth), what is the 'callback URL' used for?

options.text: It's the URL where the user enters their credentials
It's the URL the OAuth provider redirects to after the user authorizes the application, carrying an authorization code
It's the URL of the OAuth provider's API
It's the URL where error logs are sent

questions.explanation: The callback URL (redirect URI) is where the OAuth provider sends the user after they authorize your application. The provider includes an authorization code in the redirect, which your server exchanges for an access token. This URL must be registered with the OAuth provider.
